behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-queue handoff: the behavior requires both auth rejection and 'not forwarded' side effect. A unit test of only HMAC comparison could pass while the handler still enqueues on error. Real OS/network/subprocess is not required because in-process HTTP handler + queue module wiring can reproduce this. Higher levels rejected: System is disqualified by behavior text (no requirement for real network conditions/OS effects), Agentic/Workflow disqualified because no AI/ML step exists.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook route in-process with real signature-verification middleware and queue adapter module wired together; send a body with intentionally wrong X-Signature-256; assert HTTP 401 and assert no enqueue call/persisted record for that event.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering/contract failure across modules (HTTP handler and persistent queue write). A unit test of response code alone misses ordering bugs. Minimum infra is local module wiring with the real persistence-backed queue component; no real external network/OS primitive beyond local process is required. Higher levels rejected: System disqualified since behavior text does not require real external HTTP/network/subprocess; Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no model output dependency).
behaviors.test_description: Instrument the in-process route and real persistent queue module (e.g., local DB/file-backed queue) to record timestamps/checkpoints; issue a valid webhook; assert queue write commit occurs before 200 response is sent.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is about async decoupling between request path and downstream worker, i.e., module interaction and lifecycle boundary. A single-function unit test can miss accidental await/blocking in orchestration code. Real OS/network/subprocess is not necessary; controlled in-process slow worker is sufficient. Higher levels rejected: System disqualified because the behavior text is about waiting semantics, not real network/OS effects; Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI dependency).
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream worker in-process; make worker intentionally block (e.g., unresolved promise for several seconds); send valid webhook and assert response returns promptly while worker is still running/not finished.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is runtime latency under real concurrency and wall-clock constraints; mocks often hide scheduler/socket/server effects. Real HTTP server behavior and timing under concurrent load is the boundary to validate, so System is the minimum robust level. Higher levels rejected: Agentic/Workflow disqualified by behavior text (no AI/ML decisions, no agent pipeline).
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node service process and send concurrent real HTTP webhook requests while prior events trigger slow downstream processing; measure end-to-end wall-clock response times and assert acknowledgments stay under 3 seconds.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness depends on handoff/state boundary between handler and persistent queue/idempotency store. Unit-testing an ID-check helper misses real duplicate-write paths through module wiring. Real external OS/network is not inherently required if using local persistence modules. Higher levels rejected: System disqualified because behavior text only states duplicate suppression, not real network/OS conditions; Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI output dependency).
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with real dedupe/persistence modules; submit the same event ID twice; assert first request enqueues once and second request does not create a second persisted queue entry.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This verifies error-contract translation across module boundary (queue capacity signal -> HTTP status/header). Unit-testing only an error-mapper can miss real propagation path. Real OS/network/subprocess is not required; in-process queue module returning 'full' condition is enough. Higher levels rejected: System disqualified because no real network/OS failure mode is specified; Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI).
behaviors.test_description: Wire route to queue module configured to return a full-capacity condition; send a valid request; assert HTTP 503 and presence/value of Retry-After header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This is queue implementation behavior with persistence interaction; mocks can hide real capacity accounting bugs. Minimum is testing the real queue component against its real local persistence backend. System considered but rejected because behavior text requires capacity enforcement, not external network/OS primitives specifically; if backend is remote service, System would become necessary. Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI).
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue implementation with a small configured capacity on its real local storage backend; enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more; assert overflow enqueue is rejected and queue state remains at configured max.
